_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Windham County, CT. Record survival and the CT probate district system. Windham County's records are well-preserved with no major losses. The genealogical challenge is navigating French Canadian immigrant records — many families arrived from Quebec parishes where records are in French and maintained by the Catholic church, not the town clerk.

current_courthouse.note: Connecticut abolished county government in 1960. Land records at TOWN level. Probate at PROBATE DISTRICT level.

previous_courthouses.fate: Windham County was established in 1726 from Hartford and New London counties. The original county seat was at Windham (now Windham Center). No significant fire or war damage.
County seat moved to Brooklyn. Courthouse served until replaced and relocated to Putnam.

courthouse_history.critical_note_ct_probate_system: Windham County contains approximately 6 probate districts: Windham (est. 1719), Plainfield (est. 1747), Pomfret (est. 1752), Killingly (est. 1830), Putnam, and others. Each maintains independent records. The Windham Probate District (est. 1719) is the oldest and parent of most others in the county.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Windham County has experienced no significant record loss. Town records survive from the settlement period (Windham est. 1692, Plainfield est. 1699, Killingly est. 1708). The primary genealogical challenge for the industrial period is French Canadian immigrant families whose earlier records are in Quebec parish registers, not Connecticut town records. For these families, the Connecticut record begins with the first appearance in town vital records (often a marriage or a child's birth) and the trail backward leads to Quebec.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was French Canadian, their Connecticut records begin at the mill gate — marriage, children's births, and death registered with the town clerk. Everything before that is in a Quebec parish register, in French, in a different country.
record_losses.source: Kemp, Thomas Jay. Connecticut Researcher's Handbook. Detroit: Gale Research, 1981.

vital_records.completeness: Good — town records begin with settlement dates. Windham town records from 1692.
vital_records.gaps: No significant gaps. Barbour Collection covers all Windham County towns through approximately 1850.
vital_records.repository: Individual town clerks; Connecticut State Library (Barbour Collection, microfilm)
vital_records.online_access: FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Connecticut State Library digital collections

land_records.repository: Individual town clerks; Connecticut State Library (microfilm)

probate_records.completeness: Good — the Windham Probate District (est. 1719) is the parent of most others in the county
probate_records.gaps: Pre-1719 probate for Windham County towns was handled by Hartford or New London probate districts.
probate_records.repository: Individual probate courts; Connecticut State Library (microfilm)
probate_records.online_access: FamilySearch; Connecticut State Library digital collections

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1726 court records are in Hartford or New London county records.

substitute_sources.source: Barbour Collection of Connecticut Vital Records
substitute_sources.description: Comprehensive index of Connecticut vital records through approximately 1850. Covers all Windham County towns.

substitute_sources.source: Quebec Parish Registers (Programme de recherche en démographie historique — PRDH)
substitute_sources.repository: Université de Montréal (PRDH database); Drouin Collection (Ancestry.com)
substitute_sources.description: French Canadian families in Windham County's mill towns (Putnam, Danielson, Willimantic) originated in Quebec parishes. Quebec Catholic parish registers record baptisms, marriages, and burials in continuous series from the seventeenth century.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing French Canadian mill worker families back to Quebec. The PRDH database and the Drouin Collection provide access to parish registers that document the Canadian side of the family. Marriage records often name parents and places of origin.

substitute_sources.source: American Thread Company / mill company records
substitute_sources.repository: Windham Textile and History Museum (Willimantic); Connecticut State Library; University of Connecticut Archives
substitute_sources.description: Records from the American Thread Company and other Quinebaug Valley textile operations. Employment records, payroll data, and company housing records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Mill company records may document workers who left no other local records. Employment records provide dates, wages, and sometimes family information.

substitute_sources.description: Cemetery inscription transcriptions for Windham County towns.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Supplements vital records, especially for deaths before statewide registration (1897).

narrative_hooks: Windham County's records are intact — no fire, no war, no flood destroyed them. If {ancestor_name} lived here, the records survive. The challenge is knowing where to look: town clerk for births, probate district for wills, church for baptisms.

If {ancestor_name} was French Canadian, the Connecticut record begins at the mill — the marriage license, the children's births. Everything before that is in a Quebec parish register written in French by a priest who never saw Connecticut.
The Windham Probate District has been operating since 1719 — {ancestor_name}'s estate records may be among the oldest continuously maintained probate files in the nation.

Mill company records at the Windham Textile and History Museum may be the only evidence that {ancestor_name} worked at the thread mill — workers who rented company housing and shopped at the company store left few traces in town records.
source_references: Kemp, Thomas Jay. Connecticut Researcher's Handbook. Detroit: Gale Research, 1981.
Connecticut State Library. Guide to Archives in the Connecticut State Library. Hartford: Connecticut State Library, 1981.
Lavoie, Yolande. L'émigration des Québécois aux États-Unis de 1840 à 1930. Quebec: Conseil de la langue française, 1981.
